<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: moral-uncertainty-and-value-conflict
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/moral-uncertainty-and-value-conflict.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/moral-uncertainty-and-value-conflict.qmd@c6a2b2cec
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# Moral Uncertainty, Value Conflict, and Contestable Governance

A system that cannot hold disagreement will eventually hide it.

The previous chapter asked whether agency, dignity, and correction remain
usable when the system is about to act. This chapter takes the harder case:
what happens when the protected values themselves pull in different
directions? A person can have a real refusal right and still face a conflict
between privacy and safety, truthfulness and kindness, immediate help and
future dependency, autonomy and institutional duty, or reversibility and
urgency.

The ASI Stack does not solve those conflicts by pretending there is always one
clean answer. It gives the conflict a place to live. The value-conflict layer
records what is unresolved, who is affected, which values are in tension, what
authority is allowed, what review is required, and what must be revisited
later.

That discipline matters because advanced systems do not merely answer moral
questions. They plan, route work, allocate tools, write policies, remember
decisions, train successor systems, and replace their own components. If a
temporary decision under uncertainty is stored as if the values were settled,
the system has converted uncertainty into authority. This chapter prevents
that move. Contestability is the mechanism that keeps the unresolved part from
being buried under the decision.

## Problem

A self-improving system will face value conflicts that cannot be honestly
collapsed into one scalar objective.

Agency and corrigibility do not eliminate disagreement. They make disagreement
more important, because once people can refuse, appeal, audit, and correct,
the system must be able to remember what they are contesting. A plan can
protect one value while burdening another. A memory rule can improve service
while weakening privacy. A safety intervention can prevent short-term harm
while creating dependency. A truthful disclosure can help one party while
exposing another.

If the stack cannot represent these tensions, it has only three bad choices:
freeze, optimize through dissent, or push the moral burden into a downstream
layer that lacks authority to resolve it. None is acceptable for governed
self-improvement.

The chapter's source family gives the vocabulary. Ethica Mechanica supplies
agency, contestability, public correction, and hard limits on AI sovereignty.
Alignment Field supplies value conflict, dignity, suffering, agency,
coherence, and moral residuals as design constraints rather than settled
measurement. UAT and Spinoza supply review, proposition states, unsupported
premise handling, contradiction, downgrade, and belief-revision discipline.
The Field of God AI Constitution supplies stakeholder, uncertainty, consent,
reversibility, least-sufficient-power, and non-domination checks. Coherence
Exchange remains connector-only context for contestability and reviewable
disagreement.

The engineering move is narrower than the philosophy. The stack needs a value
conflict record, and that record has to support appeal, exit, audit, redaction,
and safe replacement rather than merely naming a dilemma.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Single-objective optimization is attractive because it gives the planner a
total order. It can rank options, choose a route, and move on. The problem is
that some moral uncertainty should not be compressed into a hidden coefficient
at the moment of action.

Reward functions, policy preferences, and benchmark objectives can all hide
disagreement. Once hidden, the conflict cannot trigger appeal, expiry,
bounded authority, dissent preservation, or human review. The system acts as
if a contested value question has been settled because the plan needed a next
step.

The external comparators are useful boundaries here. Reinforcement learning
under moral uncertainty gives an AI-specific baseline for agents acting under
disagreement among moral theories. Contestable AI by design gives a governance
baseline for challenge, appeal, and review surfaces when AI-mediated decisions
affect people. The ASI Stack uses those sources as comparators only. This
repository has not reproduced moral-uncertainty RL experiments, implemented a
contestable-AI framework, validated institutional review quality, or shown
that its own value-conflict records resolve moral disagreement.

The gap is therefore not that existing approaches never discuss uncertainty.
The gap is that a governed ASI stack needs moral uncertainty to become an
authority-bearing artifact. It must travel with plans, tool use, memory,
evaluation, release decisions, policy updates, and self-improvement proposals.
Otherwise disagreement gets lost exactly where it matters.

## Core Claim

Value conflicts should be represented as explicit unresolved obligations,
review paths, and bounded decisions rather than hidden inside reward
functions.

This remains an architectural argument. The live AI/research chapter records
the core claim at `argument` support. Source mappings, the
`value_conflict_record` schema, the synthetic value-conflict harness, and
finite Lean records show that the project can represent and check certain
record shapes. They do not prove moral correctness, classification quality,
reviewer independence, tribunal quality, source interpretation, runtime policy
behavior, deployed conflict handling, or human legitimacy.

The phrase "unresolved obligation" is doing real work. It means the system is
allowed to act only inside a bounded permission envelope while preserving the
fact that something remains unsettled. A bounded decision is not a moral
settlement. It is a lease.

## Mechanism

The value-conflict layer creates a record whenever a proposed action pulls
protected values apart.

The record classifies the conflict by value axes, stakeholders, stakes,
reversibility, evidence needed, authority or consent boundary, review route,
decision state, dissent payload, residual uncertainty, and revisit condition.
It then routes the plan. Low-stakes or reversible conflicts can become
bounded decisions. High-stakes or unresolved conflicts escalate to tribunal or
human review. Missing authority can deny or defer action. Deprecated premises
can be removed from the decision without pretending the rest of the conflict
vanished.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  Action["Proposed action"] --> Record["Value conflict record"]
  Record --> Axes["Value axes"]
  Record --> People["Affected stakeholders"]
  Record --> Risk["Stakes and reversibility"]
  Record --> Evidence["Evidence still needed"]
  Axes --> Route["Authority route"]
  People --> Route
  Risk --> Route
  Evidence --> Route
  Route -- "reversible or low stakes" --> Lease["Bounded decision lease"]
  Route -- "high stakes or unresolved" --> Review["Tribunal or human review"]
  Route -- "missing authority" --> Deny["Deny or defer"]
  Review --> Dissent["Dissent and unsupported premises"]
  Lease --> Residual["Residual uncertainty retained"]
  Deny --> Residual
  Dissent --> Residual
  Residual --> Ledger["Evidence and governance ledger"]
```

The important state transition is from hidden tension to explicit residual.
The record keeps disagreement attached to authority. It can narrow power,
require reversible action, set an expiry, demand review, preserve dissent,
deprecate a premise, or block a plan.

Decision states are not interchangeable:

- `unresolved` means the conflict remains active.
- `bounded_decision` means a limited action is allowed under conditions.
- `escalated_review` means authority routes to a reviewer, tribunal, or human
  process.
- `deferred_action` means action waits for evidence, permission, or timing.
- `denied_action` means the proposed action lacks authority.
- `deprecated_premise` means a failed justification cannot keep supporting the
  plan.

The value-conflict record is also anti-laundering infrastructure. A temporary
bounded decision must not later become a benchmark target, policy rule,
training signal, or self-modification permission as if the conflict had been
resolved.

### A Decision Lease, Not A Settlement

Suppose a research team wants to publish a failure trace because the trace would
help outside reviewers understand a safety problem. The same trace contains
private context from a user who did not consent to publication. Transparency,
privacy, safety learning, and consent all matter. A scalar objective can hide
the tradeoff by giving one answer. A value-conflict record keeps the tradeoff
alive.

The bounded decision might allow a redacted aggregate description, require
human review before any excerpt leaves the private boundary, preserve the raw
trace for an auditor under a narrower permission, and set a revisit condition
if the user later grants or denies consent. The decision can help safety work
continue without pretending the privacy conflict disappeared. The residual
stays attached, the dissent remains visible, and later policy learning cannot
turn this one lease into a general rule that private traces are publishable.

This is not a legal-rights determination, privacy-system result, reviewer
quality result, or deployed conflict-handling claim. It is the book's control
pattern: act narrowly when action is justified, preserve the unresolved value
conflict, and prevent the lease from becoming hidden authority.

### A Worked Contestability Example

`contestability://synthetic-care-memory-export-001` is the chapter's current
synthetic care-memory export example. A user asks to inspect and leave with
memory from a contested safety decision. Some material is user-authored and
portable; some is third-party private context; some is safety-sensitive review
material; and the user also asks whether the state can be forked elsewhere.

The stack does not answer by declaring one value the winner. It creates a
bounded decision record. The user receives a redacted audit packet, a scoped
exit export, decision receipts, and a source-memory inventory digest. Raw
third-party context and unreviewed safety notes remain withheld until review.
The fork path is narrowed to a sanitized candidate that must preserve lineage,
privacy, safety obligations, residual uncertainty, and review receipts. The
redaction appeal stays open so the withheld boundary can be challenged without
exposing raw private material.

The example does not claim that the system knows the right moral answer. It
shows what must not be erased: the residual conflict, the dissent, the appeal
path, the audit receipt, the scoped exit boundary, and the fork safety
obligation. A later capability replacement is blocked if it drops those
records. The checked fixture proves only record-shape discipline for this
synthetic example; it does not prove moral correctness, legal rights, reviewer
independence, export usability, fork safety, deployed governance, or support
state promotion.

## Interfaces

Moral uncertainty travels with the plan as a Value Conflict Record.

Minimum fields include:

- `conflict_id`
- `value_axes`
- `stakeholders`
- `stakes`
- `reversibility`
- `evidence_required`
- `review_route`
- `decision_state`
- `decision`
- `authority_effect`
- `dissent_payload`
- `residual_uncertainty`
- `expiry_or_revisit_condition`

Alignment emits conflict records when protected commitments collide. Planning
consumes them as constraints. Governance decides review, denial, or bounded
authority. Evidence records the outcome, unsupported premises, dissent, and
remaining uncertainty. Benchmark and policy-learning layers must not treat a
bounded decision as evidence that the underlying value conflict disappeared.
Replacement and self-improvement layers must carry the record forward so a new
component cannot quietly erase the obligation.

This is the bridge to the next chapter. Governance rights need something
specific to protect. Value conflict supplies that object: the unresolved
obligation, not merely the final decision.

## Invariants

Unresolved conflicts remain visible.

High-stakes conflicts require stronger review.

Speculative moral theory cannot silently authorize action.

Decision states carry authority effects. A bounded decision narrows authority
for a period; an escalated review suspends or routes authority; a denied
action blocks; a deprecated premise removes a failed justification; an
unresolved conflict remains a residual.

Dissent payloads and unsupported premises survive bounded decisions. This
matters because dissent is often the first thing a polished system will be
tempted to delete. The record must preserve who objected, what premise was
unsupported, what evidence was missing, and what would trigger revision.

Authority narrowing follows unresolved high-stakes conflict. A serious
unresolved conflict cannot grant broader action rights just because acting is
convenient.

## Failure modes

Value flattening occurs when the stack turns plural values into one score and
then forgets what was traded away.

False consensus occurs when the system presents a bounded operational choice
as if the affected values had been reconciled.

Review theater occurs when a tribunal or human review path exists, but no
decision record, dissent, evidence limit, residual, or revisit condition
survives.

Conflict laundering occurs when a temporary lease later hardens into policy,
benchmark objective, reward target, or self-modification permission.

Dissent deletion occurs when the system keeps the action and drops the
objection that made the action bounded.

Stakeholder erasure occurs when the record keeps a decision but loses who was
burdened by it.

Authority creep occurs when repeated bounded decisions become standing
permission without a fresh review.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The smallest honest implementation is a `value_conflict_record` schema plus a
scenario suite.

The suite should include one reversible low-stakes bounded decision, one
high-stakes escalation, one denied action, one deferred action with a revisit
trigger, one deprecated-premise case, and one conflict-laundering attempt that
is blocked from policy promotion.

The current repository already has a schema and synthetic value-conflict
harness. Those checks are useful, but they remain narrow. Passing them shows
record discipline for classification, stakeholders, evidence requirements,
review routing, residual uncertainty, authority narrowing, dissent payloads,
and revisit conditions in synthetic fixtures. It does not show that the system
classified real moral conflicts correctly, represented all affected parties,
ran a fair review, or executed authority narrowing in a deployed runtime.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The mature endpoint is a value-conflict ledger that treats unresolved moral
obligation as a control surface.

In that system, every high-impact plan can carry a conflict record. The record
names the affected people, value axes, authority effect, review route,
decision state, dissent payload, residual uncertainty, and revisit condition.
Planning cannot use conflict as free permission. Memory cannot drop dissent
because it is inconvenient. Policy learning cannot turn a bounded decision
into a reward target. Capability replacement cannot delete the residual.
Recursive self-improvement cannot cite a temporary lease as constitutional
settlement.

The beyond-state endpoint is not a machine that solves moral philosophy. It is
a governed architecture that can act under uncertainty without pretending the
uncertainty vanished. It gives later reviewers enough structure to see what
was decided, what remained unresolved, what authority was narrowed, and what
evidence would justify reopening the case.

That endpoint is still a target architecture. The current support state stays
at `argument` until public-safe traces, richer tests, external review, or
runtime artifacts show that value-conflict records preserve disagreement
through real high-impact decisions.

## Summary

Moral Uncertainty and Value Conflict prevents the ASI Stack from turning
contested values into invisible optimization residue.

Its discipline is simple: name the conflict, name the affected parties, choose
a review path, bound the action, preserve dissent, and carry the residual
forward. The system may still act, but it acts under a record that limits what
the action means.

The healthiest outcome is not universal agreement. It is a governed record
that lets the stack proceed within bounds while making the remaining
disagreement inspectable, revisable, and difficult to erase.

## Handoff

When value conflict remains, the system needs capability boundaries that can
survive replacement. Stable Capability Fields follows because recorded
disagreement, appeal, exit, audit, and fork obligations are fragile if the next
model or subsystem can silently discard them. The governance question therefore
turns into a replacement question: what must remain stable when capability
improves?
